attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman has just had a spontaneous vaginal delivery of her first baby. The placenta was delivered intact 10 minutes ago. She has ongoing vaginal bleeding and examination reveals a large right-sided perineal tear extending into the vaginal wall. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears tired from her recent delivery, lying down with her eyes closed. She has a well-working epidural in situ. Her pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 110/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. The uterus is firm and well-contracted on palpation. Estimated blood loss is 800 mL with ongoing heavy bleeding visualised.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with primary postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) due to genital tract trauma. The well-contracted uterus suggests that uterine atony is not the primary cause. Assessment of PPH follows the '4 Ts': tone (uterine atony), tissue (retained products), trauma (genital tract injury), and thrombin (coagulopathy).

Direct pressure to bleeding sites is the most appropriate first step in managing an actively bleeding perineal tear. This provides immediate haemorrhage control while preparing for definitive surgical repair. Direct pressure is a fundamental principle of managing any bleeding, regardless of location. Definitive management of the bleeding tear will require suturing either on the birth suite or an operating theatre depending on equipment availability and patient analgesia.

Transfer to operating theatre will possibly be required for definitive repair, but the patient requires stabilisation first. Moving an actively bleeding patient without first attempting to control the haemorrhage could worsen blood loss during transfer. In some cases perineal trauma may be more promptly sutured on a birth suite especially in the case of a well working epidural. Not all cases of PPH due to perineal trauma will need repair in the operating theatre and may be repaired in the birth suite.

Administration of oxytocin or syntometrine are both uterotonic medications used to treat uterine atony. However, in this case, the uterus is already firm and well-contracted, indicating that poor uterine tone is not contributing to the bleeding. These medications would not address the bleeding from trauma.

Insertion of a Bakri balloon is used for tamponade of uterine bleeding and would not help control bleeding from a perineal tear. It would be inappropriate in this situation where the bleeding source is clearly identified as trauma to the perineum and vaginal wall.

After applying direct pressure, the next steps should include establishing large-bore IV access, commencing fluid resuscitation and arranging urgent surgical repair.
learningPoints: Primary postpartum haemorrhage requires systematic assessment of tone, tissue, trauma and thrombin (4 Ts).
Direct pressure should be applied to actively bleeding perineal tears while preparing for definitive repair.
Blood loss estimation should include weighing of soaked materials and regular vital sign monitoring.
references: Primary postpartum haemorrhage: prevention and management [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Government; 2020 [cited 2024 Nov 21]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0015/140136/g-pph.PDF
Gunaydin B. Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage. Türk anestezi ve reanimasyon dergisi [Internet]. 2022 Dec 9;50(6):396–402. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9885818/

Clinical Guidance Statement: Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH) [Internet]. Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetrics and Gynaecology. 2021. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Management-Postpartum-Haemorrhage.pdf
